The recurrence yields P2 = 2/5, P3 = 3/7, and by a simple induction, one then checks that for general n one has Pn = n/(2n+ 1).

Note: Richard Stanley points out the following noninductive argument. Put f(x) = П½=1 x+ 2k)/(2k + 1); then the coefficient of x' in f(x) is the probability of getting exactly i heads. Thus the desired number is (f(1)- f(-1))/2, and both values of f can be computed directly: f(1) = 1, and

A-3 By the quadratic formula, if Pm (x) = 0, then x7 = m ‡ 2V 2m + 2, and hence the four roots of Pm are given by S= {Vm ‡ V2}. If Pm factors into two nonconstant polynomials over the integers, then some subset of S consisting of one or two elements form the roots of a polynomial with integer coefficients.
Note: the key relation r (1 + s) = 1 can also be derived by computing using homogeneous coordinates or vectors.

First suppose this subset has a single element, say vm ‡ V2; this element must be a rational number. Then (Vm # V2) = 2 + m 2V 2m is an integer, so m is twice a perfect square, say m = 2n. But then Vm = V2 = (n ‡ 1) V2 is only rational if n = ‡1, i.e., if m = 2.
A-5 Suppose a"+1 - (a + 1)" = 2001. Notice that a"+1 + (a + 1)" - 1 is a multiple of a; thus a divides 2002 = 2x7 × 11 × 13.

Since 2001 is divisible by 3, we must have a = 1 (mod 3), otherwise one of alt! and (a + 1)" is a multiple of 3 and the other is not, so their difference cannot be divisible by 3. Now a"+1 = 1 (mod 3), so we must have (a + 1)" = 1 (mod 3), which forces n to be even, and in particular at least 2.

Next, suppose that the subset contains two elements; then we can take it to be one of {Vm V2}, {V2+ Vm} or {#(Vm + V2)}. In all cases, the sum and the product of the elements of the subset must be a rational number. In the first case, this means 2Vm € Q, so m is a perfect square. In the second case, we have 2V2 € Q, contradiction. In the third case, we have (Vm + V2)? € Q, or m + 2 + 2V 2m € Q, which means that m is twice a perfect square.

If a is even, then a"+1 - (a + 1)"= -(a + 1)" (mod 4). Since n is even, -(a + 1)" = -1 (mod 4). Since 2001 = 1 (mod 4), this is impossible. Thus a is odd, and so must divide 1001 = 7 × 11 × 13. Moreover, a"+1 -(a+ 1)" = a (mod 4), so a = 1 (mod 4).
We conclude that Pm (x) factors into two nonconstant polynomials over the integers if and only if m is either a square or twice a square.

Of the divisors of 7 × 11 x 13, those congruent to 1 mod 3 are precisely those not divisible by 11 (since 7 and 13 are both congruent to 1 mod 3). Thus a divides 7 × 13. Now a = 1 (mod 4) is only possible if a divides 13.

Note: a more sophisticated interpretation of this argument can be given using Galois theory. Namely, if m is neither a square nor twice a square, then the number fields @(Vm) and Q(V2) are distinct quadratic fields, so their compositum is a number field of degree 4, whose Galois group acts transitively on {Im‡V2}. Thus Pm is irreducible.
